posts.text:
I thought he was still alive because he was supposed to be the Delcy Rodriguez figure who we cut a deal with? 

Ah well, nevertheless


The New York Times (@nytimes)

Breaking News: Israel believes it killed one of Iran’s most senior leaders, Ali Larijani, in overnight strikes, according to the Israeli defense minister. nyti.ms/4lyd26h




— https://nitter.net/nytimes/status/2033852222216302893#m

As YIMBYism has grown, diversified, gone mainstream, and strengthened I think the original core point that housing constraints in a handful of very high cost cities create a unique drag on the national economy has gotten a little underrated.

slowboring.com/p/why-silicon…

The Bay Area is home to five of the world’s ten largest companies but it’s not even the highest population metro area in California. 

That growth constraint is a meaningful bottleneck for the whole American economy coming. 

slowboring.com/p/why-silicon…

When Detroit was the epicenter of a “tech boom” its population soared, and industrialization drove mass prosperity. 

There’s been nothing like that with the Bay Area and the 21st century.

slowboring.com/p/why-silicon…

A lot of conservatives run this play where they’re like “wow I remember when libs thought Bush-era homophobia was bad but now they’re angry that Trump displays totally atavistic indifference to basic human decency — what hypocrites!” 

Okay, I guess, you got me. 

¯\_(ツ)_/¯


RacePope🦅🏛️⚔️🌐🏗️ (@RacePopeIII)

So wait, do you want Christian moralism or not? 

I recall people constantly bemoaning the overt Christian influence on conservative politics but now that it’s gone you want it back?
What are some good anonymous accounts?
When I was a kid we had Gerry Adams hanging out with Rudy Giuliani while the mayor condemned the British "outsider occupation force" ruling Northern Ireland.

This has to be the most right-wing take on Ireland that any NYC mayor has had since the era of Bill the Butcher.


Timmy Facciola (@TimmyFacciola_)

Zohran Mamdani on whether he supports a united Ireland:

"I gotta be honest, I haven't thought enough on that question."

Video
  





— https://nitter.net/TimmyFacciola_/status/2033608546156270059#m

Some people would do well to reflect a little bit internally on what it does to the culture and overall epistemic and even moral environment when powerful, high-status people decide to get slippery and defensive rather than just admit they overstated things on a podcast.
Conservative politics untempered by any sense of honor or Christian moralism is really ugly.
Cannot believe they closed schools for whatever the weather was today.

I'm not going to be like "back home in Greenwich Village what people REALLY care about is the viability of standalone movie theaters like The Quad and Cinema Village in a world dominated by Regal & AMC," though I do know people like this.

I think one of my strengths as a pundit is that in my personal life I have always been so comically out of touch with real america that I don't make the slightest effort to generalize based on my personal experiences and just seek out objective research.


Zaid Jilani (@ZaidJilani)

Matt is correct about the problem, although as someone who has only done politics in the mean streets of Manhattan, Cambridge, and Washington DC he might not be exactly the purple-red state whisperer for the solution

Some members of the Slow Boring team are suggesting that today's article would have had a higher open rate if they headline wasn't keyed into the distinction in Marxist theory between a "class in itself" and a "class for itself."

slowboring.com/p/upscale-lib…

I opted not to try to shoehorn this into my NYT piece but yes...


Jonathan Robinson (@jon_m_rob)

Buried in Appendix F of the new @dbroockman & @j_kalla paper -- voters don’t seem to care whether candidates crack down on monopolies (or might even prefer candidates who let more mergers go through 👀!)





— https://nitter.net/jon_m_rob/status/2033625511478399415#m

TBH I think "Open Society And Its Enemies" (which despite the slightly bizarre decision to engage in a smear campaign against Plato is basically correct in its upshot) holds up a lot better than the philosophy of science.


Jesús Fernández-Villaverde (@JesusFerna7026)

I must admit that, among all the thinkers I admired when I was 20, none has fallen further in my estimation than Karl Popper. I will leave aside his contributions to the philosophy of science and speak only about The Open Society

Democrats need to think in terms of fiscal priorities — if you do a huge across the board tax cut it then becomes very difficult to restore Trump's Medicaid cuts and borderline impossible to invest in any new programs.  

slowboring.com/p/upscale-lib…

The YIMBY movement has come to encompass a wide range of themes and ideas — single stair, missing middle, manufactured homes — and achieved national scope but the core question of growth (or lack thereof) in a handful of very high cost areas remains crucial. 

www.slowboring.com/p/why-silico...
In my defense, the text was wrapped up days ago
If I had a barrel of West Texas Intermediate crude oil laying around somewhere, I would not sell it for $100 given the current circumstances.
I think Caraballo is just restating the problem here not offering an alternative solution.

Today in the NYT, I discuss some fresh research on which moderations move the most voters.

www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/o...
This is an amazing platform

If per person spending on the elderly goes up and the elderly share of the population also goes up, everything else gets squeezed and everyone is mad.

But it’s what the voters say they want.

www.slowboring.com/p/the-demoni...

A defense of the unfairly maligned “corporate landlord” — there’s no reason rental housing should be all owned by small-time landlords, this isn’t like the local coffee shop that’s a treasured neighborhood resource.

www.slowboring.com/p/maybe-all-...
When I wrote down "valley" as a potential story idea, what do you think I meant?

Successful military adventures like the recent one in Venezuela beget hubris and set the stage for new fiascos as arrogant leaders forget that you can’t just count on getting lucky all the time.

www.slowboring.com/p/nobody-pla...

“Stop AOC” is a terrible mission for 2028

www.slowboring.com/p/aoc-is-not...

There are levels of NIMBYism where even detached single family homes aren’t acceptable 

www.bangordailynews.com/2026/03/05/h...

From 50,000 feet I think it's easy to post on the internet "we need to return to real state capacity like in the New Deal instead of this endless reliance on contracting and outsourcing." 

But contracting is a very convenient workaround to public sector pay compression, unions, job tenure, etc
Very good points here, though I question whether progressives are prepared to really do what it would take to reduce dependence on these kind of contractors.

Want to get out ahead of this and concede that Yglesias Thought may be totally discredited as it turns out that "don't change anything, hope Trump just fucks up spectacularly in a way that sinks Republicans" works fine as a plan.
Platner dining table is 25% off this week

It’s very unpleasant to be subjected to an insane internet pile-on, but I think many writers end up sabotaging themselves by trying too hard to avoid the inevitable.

www.slowboring.com/p/everyone-g...
Not sure these guys have thought this through

War unpopular, Trump blamed for high prices, Democrats have a nice lead in generic ballot, Republicans still more trusted on immigration.

www.nbcnews.com/politics/202...
Why Silicon Valley hasn’t done more for most Americans (by Matthew Yglesias)
Quinceañeras and Republican tumult (by Halina Bennet)
Upscale liberals are developing class consciousness (by Matthew Yglesias)
Sunday Thread + Mailbag (by Halina Bennet)
Saturday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Could the Second Mexican Empire have endured? (by Matthew Yglesias). Plus the case for proportional representation, Obama’s foreign policy, and an S-Bahn for Greater Boston
I don’t buy your “dating recession” (by Halina Bennet). The collapse of romantic infrastructure is an engineering failure, not a Gen Z character flaw.
The demonic policy strangling the British economy  (by Matthew Yglesias)
Thursday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Maybe *all* rental housing should be owned by large institutional investors (by Matthew Yglesias)

One bad provision could sink a critical bipartisan housing bill  (by Halina Bennet). Despite sweeping support and real substance, a poorly drafted investor ban could kill the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act.
Nobody plans for a quagmire  (by Matthew Yglesias). Behind every military disaster is an honest mistake.
Tuesday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
A.O.C. is not the problem (by Matthew Yglesias). Centrist Democrats need to push for change, not bandwagon behind the broken establishment.
First primaries, first lessons (by Halina Bennet). Plus, more Republican retirements and Trump’s Iran problem
